---
title: "Finding the Features That Matter: Filters, Trees, and Forests"
unit: "IV"
lesson: "11"
type: concept
tags: [feature-selection, chi-square, decision-trees, random-forests, classification]
difficulty: introductory
duration: "50 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Explain** why selecting a few informative features beats keeping thousands of weak ones. *(Understand)*
- **Rank** features with a chi-square filter score and **interpret** the top of the ranking. *(Apply)*
- **Train** a shallow decision tree on selected features, **read** its splits in plain language, and **compare** its accuracy against the full feature set and an honest baseline. *(Apply / Analyze)*
- **Describe** how a random forest aggregates many randomized trees, and **judge** the trade between its accuracy and a single tree's readability. *(Understand / Analyze)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L10** built a 5,574 x 2,003 feature matrix from raw text · closes Unit IV (extracting information from data) · next, Unit V turns to *showing* patterns — visualization.

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — From 2,003 Features to the 30 That Matter](l11_lab_feature_selection_spam.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u04_data_mining/l11_lab_feature_selection_spam.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

In L10 you turned 5,574 text messages into a feature matrix with **2,003 columns**.
Now your boss asks the obvious question: *"So — what makes a message spam?"*

You cannot answer with 2,003 numbers. Most of those columns are noise: words that
appear a handful of times, words that appear equally in both classes, words that
duplicate what another column already says. Somewhere in the matrix, a small set of
features carries nearly all the signal. Finding it is called **feature selection**,
and it is the skill this unit's title promises: *extracting information* from data.

The payoff is concrete. By the end of the paired lab, a decision rule small enough
to read aloud — three questions about a message — classifies spam almost exactly as
well as the full 2,003-column matrix. And you will know precisely *which* features
earned their place.

## What Is Feature Selection?

> **Analogy:** a detective canvassing an apartment building interviews two hundred
> residents, but the case turns on two witnesses. The skill is not collecting more
> statements — it is recognizing which two matter.

**Feature selection** is choosing a subset of the existing columns to keep, and
discarding the rest. It is the complement of L10's skill: feature *engineering*
invented columns; feature *selection* decides which ones deserve to stay. Three
reasons to do it:

- **Interpretability.** Thirty features can be inspected, plotted, and explained
  to a boss. Two thousand cannot.
- **Noise.** Useless columns do not just sit quietly — an algorithm searching for
  patterns can find accidental ones in them. Fewer, better features means fewer
  chances to be fooled.
- **Cost.** Every column must be computed, stored, and maintained for every new
  observation, forever. A feature that earns nothing still bills you.

To *select* the best features, though, we need a way to *score* them — and the most
honest scorecard is how well they support a prediction. That requires a short detour.

## A Short Detour: Teaching the Machine to Decide

This is the course's first contact with **supervised classification**, and we take
just enough of it to score feature sets — five terms:

- The **label** is the answer column: `ham` or `spam`, recorded for every message
  in our collection. Supervised means *the answers are known* for the data we
  learn from.
- A **classifier** is an algorithm that learns, from labeled examples, a rule that
  maps a feature vector to a predicted label. In scikit-learn the two verbs are
  `fit` (learn from examples) and `predict` (apply the learned rule to new ones).
- A **train/test split** divides the data — typically 70% for fitting, 30% held
  back for testing. The rule is graded **only on messages it never saw**, exactly
  like an exam made of questions that were not on the study guide. Grading on the
  training data rewards memorization, and memorization does not transfer.
- **Accuracy** is the fraction of test messages classified correctly.
- A **baseline** is the accuracy of the laziest possible strategy. Our dataset is
  13.4% spam, so the rule "predict *ham* for everything" already scores **86.6%**.

::: {.callout-important title="Accuracy starts at the baseline, not at zero"}
Whenever classes are imbalanced, quote the majority-class baseline next to any
accuracy. A spam filter scoring 90% sounds impressive until you know that doing
*nothing* scores 86.6%. The interesting quantity is the distance above the
baseline, not the distance above zero.
:::

That is the whole detour. Now we can score features: a feature set is good if a
classifier built on it predicts held-out labels well — or, even before any
classifier exists, if the feature's values move visibly with the label. That
"moves with the label" idea is the first family.

## Family 1: Filters — Score Each Feature on Its Own

A **filter method** scores every feature *independently* against the label, ranks
them, and keeps the top *k*. No classifier is involved — it is a statistical test
applied 2,003 times. You have seen this move before: in L08, Pearson's *r* scored
the relationship between two numeric variables with one number. A filter score
does the same job for *feature versus label*.

The classic filter for count data is the **chi-square ($\chi^2$) score**. Its question:
*does this feature appear differently across the classes than chance would
predict?* Take the word "free" in the real corpus:

| | contains "free" | does not | total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **spam** | **170** | 577 | 747 |
| **ham** | 59 | 4,768 | 4,827 |
| **total** | 229 | 5,345 | 5,574 |

If "free" ignored the label, spam's share of it would match spam's share of the
corpus: $229 \times (747 / 5574) \approx$ **31 spam messages** would contain "free". We observe
**170** — more than five times the expected count. The chi-square statistic adds up
gaps like that (squared, scaled by the expected counts) across the table's cells;
for this table it comes to roughly **760**. A word distributed indifferently to the
label scores near **zero** — in the lab, the word "email" scores 0.000 to three
decimals, meaning it appears at almost exactly the same rate in spam and ham and
is useless for telling them apart.

*(scikit-learn's `chi2` scorer applies the same idea to the count values themselves
rather than to presence/absence, and the lab computes it on the training split
only — so its numbers differ in detail, but the ranking logic is identical.)*

Filters are fast and scale to any width — scoring 2,003 features takes milliseconds.
Their blind spot: each feature is judged **alone**, so a pair of features that only
work *in combination* will both score poorly. Catching interactions requires a
method that considers features together — which is exactly what a tree does.

## Family 2: Decision Trees — Selection by Questioning

A **decision tree** classifies by asking a sequence of yes/no questions about the
features, one per node, until it reaches a leaf that names a class. Here is a tree
a human might build for six toy messages:

| message | digit_count | contains "prize" | label |
|---|---|---|---|
| "ok see you at 5" | 1 | no | ham |
| "running late sorry" | 0 | no | ham |
| "FREE prize waiting claim now" | 0 | yes | spam |
| "call 09061701461 to collect" | 11 | no | spam |
| "are you free for lunch" | 0 | no | ham |
| "win cash text WIN to 81010" | 5 | no | spam |

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A{"digit_count > 4 ?"} -->|yes| B["spam (2 of 2 correct)"]
    A -->|no| C{"contains 'prize' ?"}
    C -->|yes| D["spam (1 of 1 correct)"]
    C -->|no| E["ham (3 of 3 correct)"]
```

**Reading the output:** the root question alone (`digit_count > 4?`) cleanly
captures the two number-stuffed spam messages; one more question (`contains
"prize"?`) catches the digit-free spam. Notice the tree did *not* ask about "free",
even though L10 found it spam-flavored — "are you free for lunch" is ham, so on
these six messages "free" misfires where "prize" does not. The tree found that out
by itself.

How? At each node the algorithm tries **every feature and every threshold** and
keeps the question whose answer best separates the classes — the split that leaves
the two resulting groups as *pure* (single-class) as possible. Then it repeats
inside each group, until the groups are pure or a **depth** limit stops it. Depth
is the honesty knob: a depth-3 tree asks at most three questions per message, so
whatever it learns must be expressible in three questions.

That mechanism makes the tree a feature selector for free: **the features it
spends its questions on are the selection.** Give it 2,003 candidates and a depth
of 3, and the handful of features appearing in its nodes are the ones that earned
a place.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Deep Dive: how 'purity' is measured (optional)"}
The standard purity score is **entropy**: $H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$, summed
over the classes. A group that is all one class has $H = 0$ (perfectly pure); a
50/50 group has $H = 1$ (maximum confusion). A 75/25 split sits in between:

$$
H = -\left(0.75 \log_2 0.75 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25\right) = 0.811
$$

At each node the tree picks the split that most *reduces* entropy from parent to
children (the "information gain"). scikit-learn's default uses a close cousin of
entropy called the Gini impurity — same intuition, slightly cheaper to compute.
:::

## Family 3: Random Forests — Many Trees, One Vote

One tree is readable but jumpy: a slightly different training sample can change an
early split and reshuffle everything below it. A **random forest** embraces that
instability — it grows hundreds of trees, each on a random resample of the training
data and a random subset of features at each split, and classifies by **majority
vote**. Individual trees' quirks cancel; the consensus is usually more accurate
than any single tree.

The forest also yields a second feature ranking: averaging how much each feature's
splits purify the data, across all the trees, gives a **feature importance** score.
In the lab, the forest's importance ranking and the chi-square filter — two methods
with nothing in common internally — crown the same leaders. When independent
methods agree, believe them.

The price is the thing this lesson is about: **you can no longer read the model.**
Two hundred trees voting is not an explanation. The forest buys accuracy by
giving up exactly what made the single tree valuable.

## Does Selecting Cost Accuracy?

Here are the real numbers from the lab, on the held-out 30% of messages:

| Model | Features used | Test accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Majority-class baseline | 0 | 86.6% |
| Decision tree, depth 3 | all 2,003 | 98.0% |
| Decision tree, depth 3 | top 30 (chi-square) | 97.8% |
| Random forest, 200 trees | all 2,003 | 99.1% |

**Reading the output:** dropping 1,973 of the 2,003 features cost the tree **0.2
percentage points**. Thirty columns do the work of two thousand — the signal was
that concentrated. And this is the depth-3 tree the top-30 features buy:

![The lab's depth-3 decision tree drawn as a diagram: the root node splits on digit_count at 4.5; its left branch asks about the words www and mobile, and its right branch refines on digit_count at 9.5 and message length near 180 characters. Leaves are colored orange for ham and blue for spam.](assets/l11_spam_tree.png)

**Reading the output:** the root split is `digit_count <= 4.5` — the feature you
*handcrafted* in L10 beat all 2,000 vocabulary columns to the most valuable
position in the tree. Messages with five or more digits are essentially spam
territory (refined by length); nearly digit-free messages are ham unless words
like "www" or "mobile" appear. That is a spam filter you can read aloud in one
breath — at 97.8% accuracy.

## When to Use Which

- **Filter (chi-square ranking)** — first move on any wide matrix: cheap, fast,
  model-free triage from thousands of candidates down to dozens. Use it when
  features should each carry signal alone; it cannot see interactions.
- **Single shallow tree** — when the deliverable is an *explanation*: a readable
  rule, a diagram for a meeting, a policy a human signs off on. Slightly less
  accurate, infinitely more accountable.
- **Random forest** — when accuracy is the priority and a ranked feature list is
  explanation enough; also the standard second opinion on a filter's ranking.
- **Avoid selection entirely** when you have few features already (L09's twelve
  Ames columns need no triage) — or when a regulation or scientific question
  requires keeping a specific variable regardless of its score.

## Common Pitfalls

- **Selecting before splitting.** If you rank features on *all* the data and then
  split for testing, the test set already leaked its preferences into the
  selection — your accuracy is gently inflated and you will not notice. An
  earlier version of this course's spam project made exactly this mistake. Fit
  the selector on the training split only, like any other learned step.
- **Chi-square needs non-negative features.** It reasons about counts, so columns
  holding negative values (differences, values centered at zero) break its math —
  scikit-learn will refuse. Counts, lengths, and 0/1 indicators are all safe; for general
  numeric features other filter scores exist.
- **Importance is not causation.** "digit_count ranks first" means it *separates
  the classes in this collection*, not that digits cause spam. A spammer who
  spells out "oh-eight-hundred" defeats the feature without changing what the
  message is.
- **Correlated features split the credit.** `length` and `digit_count` partly
  overlap (digit-heavy messages run long); a forest divides importance between
  them. A feature ranked 15th might be a casualty of redundancy, not uselessness —
  drop one twin and the other's importance jumps.

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- Given a scenario (2,000 features vs 12 features; explanation needed vs not), decide whether feature selection is called for and why <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- Given a small word-by-class count table, pick the word a chi-square filter would rank highest (and the one it would score near zero) <!-- obj 2 · Apply · Moodle MC -->
- Given a printed two-level tree, classify a described message by walking the splits <!-- obj 3 · Apply · Moodle MC -->
- The forest scores 99.1%, the depth-3 tree 97.8%: pick the right model for a stated goal (audit/explanation vs raw accuracy) and justify <!-- obj 4 · Analyze · Moodle MC -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"Why not just keep all 2,003 features? Storage is cheap."** — Storage is cheap;
*attention* is not. Every kept feature is a column someone must compute for every
future message, a knob that can drift or break, and a chance for the model to
learn an accidental pattern. The lab's forest rates 1,280 of the 2,003 features
below 0.0001 importance — most of that matrix is dead weight being hauled around.

**"The forest got 99.1% — are we done?"** — Not yet, for two honest reasons.
First, accuracy treats every mistake as equal — but silently deleting a message
from your mother and letting one ad through are *not* equal mistakes. On
imbalanced classes you need scores that separate the two error types (they are
called precision and recall — the natural next step, not taught here). Second,
"done" depends on the deliverable: in many settings the *explanation is the
product* — a bank declining a loan, a spam policy a regulator audits — and a
readable rule that gives up one point of accuracy is the better engineering
decision. Know what you are actually optimizing before celebrating the number.

**"Is this machine learning?"** — Yes — `fit`, `predict`, train/test: you have
just done supervised learning. This course stops at using classifiers to *extract
and validate information* (which features matter, what the rule says); the craft
of modeling itself — tuning, validation, the menu of algorithms — fills a course
of its own.

## Cheat-sheet

| Situation | Move | Call |
|---|---|---|
| Thousands of candidate features | filter-rank, keep top *k* | `SelectKBest(chi2, k=...)` |
| Need an honest score for a feature set | train/test split + accuracy vs baseline | `train_test_split`, `.score` |
| Deliverable is a readable rule | shallow tree, then read it | `DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3)`, `export_text` |
| Accuracy first, list of drivers second | random forest + importances | `RandomForestClassifier`, `.feature_importances_` |
| Tempted to select on all the data | don't — fit the selector on train only | `SelectKBest(...).fit(X_train, y_train)` |

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [scikit-learn — Feature selection](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/feature_selection.html) — SelectKBest, chi2, and the model-based selectors this lesson previews.
- **Book chapter:** [Python Data Science Handbook — In-Depth: Decision Trees and Random Forests](https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook/05.08-random-forests.html) (VanderPlas) — the tree/forest mechanics with visuals.
- **The dataset's paper:** Almeida, T. A., Gomez Hidalgo, J. M., & Yamakami, A. (2011). "Contributions to the Study of SMS Spam Filtering: New Collection and Results", *ACM DocEng 2011* — what accuracy is achievable on this corpus with heavier machinery.
